{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Delivery ETA presentation is being changed from exact timestamps ('Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') to date-only windows ('Delivery by Friday'). This affects how customers perceive delivery commitments and how the organization measures on-time performance.",
    "bounded_context": "Delivery Tracking / Customer-Facing ETA",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "ETA", "definition": "Estimated Time of Arrival — the commitment shown to the customer for when their delivery will arrive" },
      { "term": "Delivery Window", "definition": "The time range within which a delivery is expected to occur" },
      { "term": "On-Time Delivery (OTD)", "definition": "A delivery that arrives within the committed ETA — the definition of 'on-time' changes depending on whether the commitment is timestamp-precision or date-precision" },
      { "term": "SLA", "definition": "Service Level Agreement — contractual delivery time commitments, often with penalties for breach" },
      { "term": "Last Mile", "definition": "The final leg of delivery from distribution hub to recipient" },
      { "term": "Exact Timestamp ETA", "definition": "Current format: a specific date+time commitment (e.g., 14:30 on Friday)" },
      { "term": "Date-Only Window ETA", "definition": "Proposed format: a date-level commitment with no time specificity (e.g., Friday)" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "An ETA, once shown to a customer, constitutes a delivery promise. Changing the precision of that promise changes the contract with the customer.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Customer trust is built on the accuracy and honesty of delivery commitments. If the displayed ETA is a promise, degrading its precision is a material change to the customer relationship.",
      "testable_assertion": "The ETA format displayed to the customer must match the precision level that the fulfillment system can reliably deliver against."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "On-Time Delivery (OTD) metrics must be measured against the same precision as the customer-facing commitment. If ETA is date-only, OTD is measured at date granularity.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Measuring OTD at a different precision than what was promised creates a disconnect between operational metrics and customer experience. The system's definition of 'on time' would be corrupt.",
      "testable_assertion": "If ETA is 'Friday', a delivery at 23:59 on Friday is on-time. If ETA is '14:30 Friday', a delivery at 14:31 is late."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "Any change to ETA precision must not violate existing SLA contracts with B2B customers that specify time-level delivery guarantees.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "SLA contracts are legal obligations. Unilaterally reducing ETA precision may constitute a breach of contract if time-level guarantees are stipulated.",
      "testable_assertion": "All active SLA contracts must be audited; any contract specifying time-level delivery guarantees must continue to receive time-level ETAs."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "ETA precision may vary by service tier — premium/express services should retain higher-precision ETAs than standard services.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a business policy decision. A VP could decide all tiers get date-only or all tiers get timestamps. It is not a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a customer on a premium tier, the ETA displayed should be at timestamp precision. Given a customer on standard tier, the ETA may be date-only."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Downstream systems consuming ETA data (notifications, driver dispatch, warehouse scheduling) must not break when ETA precision changes.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If internal systems expect timestamp-precision data and receive date-only data, scheduling and dispatch logic could produce corrupt or nonsensical results.",
      "testable_assertion": "All systems consuming ETA data must be identified and verified to handle date-only values without error or degraded behavior."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A customer viewing their delivery status for a standard-tier order",
      "when": "The ETA is displayed",
      "then": "The ETA shows a date-only window (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday') with no time component"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A B2B customer with an SLA contract specifying time-level delivery guarantees",
      "when": "The ETA is displayed",
      "then": "The ETA retains timestamp precision to honor the contractual obligation"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A delivery that arrives at any time on the promised date",
      "when": "The OTD metric is calculated",
      "then": "The delivery is counted as on-time"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "The ETA format has changed to date-only",
      "when": "Notification systems, driver dispatch, and warehouse scheduling consume the ETA",
      "then": "All downstream systems function correctly without errors, using appropriate internal timestamps for their own scheduling"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A customer who previously received timestamp ETAs",
      "when": "They now see date-only ETAs",
      "then": "No increase in support ticket volume exceeding 10% above baseline within 30 days of rollout"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Delivery date is today — 'Delivery by Today' gives the customer zero planning information about whether to stay home now or leave and come back", "expected_behavior": "Same-day deliveries should retain a time window or at minimum a half-day indicator (AM/PM) to preserve customer utility" },
    { "scenario": "Cross-timezone deliveries where 'Friday' is ambiguous — sender is in US Pacific, recipient is in US Eastern, warehouse is in Central", "expected_behavior": "Date-only ETA must be anchored to the recipient's local timezone, clearly stated" },
    { "scenario": "Date boundary: a delivery arriving at 00:05 Saturday when the ETA was 'Friday'", "expected_behavior": "This is a late delivery. The system must handle the boundary correctly and not round or fudge dates." },
    { "scenario": "Customer has scheduled time off work specifically around the old timestamp ETA", "expected_behavior": "Transition communication must be clear. Customers accustomed to planning around exact times will feel a loss of control." },
    { "scenario": "API consumers and third-party integrations that parse ETA as a datetime field", "expected_behavior": "API response schema change from datetime to date is a breaking change. Must be versioned or communicated with deprecation notice." },
    { "scenario": "Historical reporting: comparing OTD rates before and after the change", "expected_behavior": "OTD rates will artificially improve since the window is wider. Historical metrics must be annotated to prevent false conclusions about operational improvement." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Mixed. Reduced customer support costs from fewer 'where is my delivery' calls triggered by missed hour-level ETAs. However, risk of revenue loss from customer churn if perceived as service degradation, especially for premium-paying customers. OTD metrics will appear to improve (wider window = easier to meet), which may mask operational inefficiencies that cost money. Potential SLA penalty exposure if B2B contracts are not audited.",
    "operational": "Positive for operations in the short term: less pressure on last-mile scheduling to hit exact time slots, reduced driver route optimization complexity. Negative: loss of granular ETA feedback loop means operations loses a signal for identifying systemic delays. OTD dashboards become less useful for pinpointing time-of-day bottlenecks. Internal dispatch and warehouse systems may still need timestamp-level data even if customer-facing display is date-only — creating a dual-precision data model.",
    "user": "Negative for end customers. Timestamp ETAs let customers plan their day — take a lunch break, schedule a meeting, decide whether to work from home. Date-only ETAs force customers to be available all day or risk missing the delivery. This is a meaningful reduction in customer utility and autonomy. Competitor services offering time-window ETAs (e.g., '2-hour window') will have a UX advantage. Same-day delivery UX degrades severely.",
    "risk": "HIGH. (1) SLA breach risk if B2B contracts specify time-level guarantees. (2) Customer satisfaction regression — NPS/CSAT impact likely. (3) API breaking change for third-party integrators. (4) Metric gaming — artificially inflated OTD rates may hide real operational problems. (5) Competitive disadvantage — industry trend is toward MORE precision (2-hour windows, live tracking), not less.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "End Customers (B2C)", "impact": "Loss of delivery time visibility. Cannot plan their day. Higher likelihood of missed deliveries if not home. Perceived service degradation." },
      { "group": "B2B / Enterprise Customers", "impact": "Potential SLA contract violation if time-level guarantees exist. Downstream impact on their own operations (e.g., a retailer planning staff around incoming inventory)." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Short-term: fewer complaints about missed exact-time ETAs. Medium-term: likely increase in 'when will it actually arrive?' inquiries since date-only gives no intra-day guidance." },
      { "group": "Operations / Dispatch", "impact": "Reduced pressure to hit time slots. But loss of a valuable signal — if nobody is measuring against time-precision, time-precision problems go undetected." },
      { "group": "Product / Growth", "impact": "Competitive disadvantage. Industry is moving toward tighter windows and real-time tracking. Going to date-only is moving against market direction." },
      { "group": "Data / Analytics", "impact": "OTD metrics become incomparable across the transition. Historical trend analysis breaks. Must maintain two measurement regimes." },
      { "group": "Third-Party API Consumers", "impact": "Breaking schema change from datetime to date. Requires API versioning, migration period, and partner communication." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 52,
    "reasoning": "The proposal is underspecified. Critical information is missing: (1) Why is this change being made — is it because current ETAs are inaccurate and eroding trust, or is it a simplification play? (5) What does the API contract look like today? Without the motivation and scope, I cannot confidently distinguish between 'this is an honest recalibration of a broken promise' and 'this is a gratuitous loss of customer value.'",
    "high_confidence_areas": [

      "SLA contracts must be audited before any change (BR-3)",
      "Same-day delivery is a critical edge case that date-only handles poorly",
      "API schema change is breaking and requires versioning",
      "Customer utility decreases when time precision decreases"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Whether the motivation is accuracy improvement (current ETAs are unreliable) or simplification — this changes the entire analysis",
      "Whether B2B SLA contracts with time-level guarantees exist",
      "Which service tiers and delivery types are in scope",
      "Whether the internal ETA calculation engine is also changing or just the display layer",
      "Net financial impact direction — depends entirely on current ETA accuracy rates"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The change affects customer-facing display, not internal operational scheduling",
      "Both B2C and B2B customers are in scope unless stated otherwise",
      "Same-day delivery exists as a service offering",
      "Third-party API consumers exist and currently receive datetime-precision ETAs"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "WHY is this change being made? If current timestamp ETAs are frequently wrong (e.g., only 40% accurate), then moving to date-only is an honesty improvement that INCREASES trust.     }

  "rejection_reasons": [

    { "violation": "Proposal does not address existing B2B SLA contracts that may specify time-level delivery guarantees", "business_rule_broken": "BR-3 — SLA contracts are legal obligations that cannot be unilaterally degraded." },
    { "violation": "No mention of API versioning strategy for consumers currently receiving datetime-precision ETAs", "business_rule_broken": "BR-5 — downstream systems must not break when ETA precision changes." }
